What is involved in manufacturing? Planning. You don't just manufacture a car - you build blueprints. There are parts and pieces and pieces. Allah ﷻ knows which one of you has game, has a game plan. First you're going to look at her, then you're going to wink, then she's going to think you have a bug in your eye, and you're going to go over and say, "Are you on Facebook? I just wanted to let you know because I want to invite you to future Islamic events. Please take my Facebook."

Then it goes further. The guy's got a whole plan laid out. He's going to start an innocent conversation, ask for homework: "Do you have yesterday's assignment? I was wondering if you have it." There are 300 students in the class. Why did you go to her? Why did you go to her to ask?

You're manufacturing an entire strategy. "Today I'll talk to her once. Tomorrow, twice. Then I might say, 'Hey, I'm going to go get some pizza. You want some?' Then next time I'll say, 'Hey, you want to come along? Want to go for a walk? Aren't you in that other class?' - that I just signed up for because I knew you signed up for it."

He's got a whole plan, and she says, "Oh, what a coincidence! SubhanAllah, I had no idea!" She's oblivious.

Some girls are sharp, but then there are some girls whose innocence is annoying. They have no idea what's going on. It's ridiculous. "He was just asking me for my homework." What planet do you live on? "This guy in college, he's asking a lot of questions about Islam. Is it okay if I talk to him at Starbucks? We talked for four hours yesterday about Sharia, and today he wants to know about Aqeedah. He's got so many questions. Should I talk to him?" No. "But he says he's more comfortable talking to me." "Next week he was talking to me about Islam and he said if he converts, would I marry him? I don't mind, but do you think that's okay?"

I get those questions all the time. The guy manufactures an entire plan. It's not just one thing - he's building a factory literally. Plans go into this.

Girls should not be naive to that. They should learn to keep a stern voice. A stern voice scares the wits out of a guy. "Hey, so are you on Facebook?" "Who wants to know?" It's over. He's not going to mess with you again. It's over. Done. You won't have that problem again.

All women have to do is have a stern voice. The scariest thing to a man is the stern voice of his mother, his sister, his wife. "When are you coming home?" - as opposed to "When are you coming home?" There's a world of difference.

Keep your voice sharp. You have to be tough. Girls naturally - I have four daughters, I know - girls are just giggly, smiley. They don't even have a reason to smile. My girls and their friends get together and they don't even talk. They just look at each other and laugh.

This is important. Why? Because when a guy sees a girl smile, what happens to him? He gets a picture in the back of his head: "Oh yeah. She doesn't even realize what she's doing. She has no idea, no clue, the effect she has. Her smile is magical." He looks drugged. She doesn't even know.

Now he's going to plan and scheme. "I love to make her laugh," she says. "I talked to him because he's funny." إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ خَبِيرٌۢ بِمَا يَصْنَعُونَ - Allah knows all of this stuff.

If you lowered your eyes, you wouldn't have been hypnotized by her smile. If you weren't hypnotized by her smile, you wouldn't start concocting the entire plan, changing your course schedule, signing up for all the classes where she is, somehow becoming her lab partner. You wouldn't have done all of that. You would have saved yourself and another believer from a lot of Shaytan, from a lot of sin.

But this is what happens. And SubhanAllah, we have the blessing of Facebook where we can put all kinds of pictures - religious boys and girls, group photos at the MSA - so you could just stare at her smile now for hours. You can do that.

"You're too extreme. Take it easy." I'd rather stay extreme. I'm sorry, I have daughters to worry about. I think they'll survive without Facebook.

I joined Facebook because I wanted to get in touch with my cousins from Pakistan. I left Pakistan in 1993 and haven't gone back. I joined Facebook and got hundreds, then thousands of friend requests. Even the phrase "friend request" bothered me. "I don't know this girl. What are you saying? You want to be my friend?" I don't even like the language.

So I just stopped. Even if I befriended a brother, it would take me to his page and there are all these other pictures. Why? Why are you doing this? Why do you have a picture of you hugging your wife? That's not right. There's something wrong with that. This is supposed to be behind closed doors. Why do you think Allah ﷻ said to knock first? Why are you putting it on public display now? You don't even have to knock - just click and you've entered people's homes, the very thing Allah ﷻ didn't want us to do. We said, "Okay, we'll maintain the knocking part, but we'll just take everything that's supposed to be private and put it on the web for the world to see." It's absurd.

I'm not a mufti. I can't tell you what's halal and haram. I'm not going to pass a fatwa that Facebook is haram or Twitter is haram. I'm saying use it responsibly. These devices have brought a lot of good to the world. A lot of transparency has been brought to journalism. Revolutions, popular uprisings that couldn't have been imagined came about through Twitter and Facebook. It's good stuff, but use it responsibly. These teachings are not a joke. They have to clean up our practices.

translation: And tell the believing women to reduce [some] of their vision and guard their private parts and not expose their adornment except that which [necessarily] appears thereof and to wrap [a portion of] their headcovers over their chests and not expose their adornment except to their husbands, their fathers, their husbands' fathers, their sons, their husbands' sons, their brothers, their brothers' sons, their sisters' sons, their women, that which their right hands possess, or those male attendants having no physical desire, or children who are not yet aware of the private aspects of women. And let them not stamp their feet to make known what they conceal of their adornment. And turn to Allah in repentance, all of you, O believers, that you might succeed
commentary:
وَقُل لِّلْمُؤْمِنَـٰتِ يَغْضُضْنَ مِنْ أَبْصَـٰرِهِنَّ وَيَحْفَظْنَ فُرُوجَهُنَّ وَلَا يُبْدِينَ زِينَتَهُنَّ إِلَّا مَا ظَهَرَ مِنْهَا ۖ وَلْيَضْرِبْنَ بِخُمُرِهِنَّ عَلَىٰ جُيُوبِهِنَّ...

"And tell the believing women to lower their gaze and guard their chastity, and not to display their adornment except what naturally appears thereof, and to draw their headcovers over their chests..."

Notice the immediate parallel to the previous ayah. Just as believing men were commanded to lower their gaze, believing women are given the exact same directive. يَغْضُضْنَ مِنْ أَبْصَـٰرِهِنَّ—the "min" (مِنْ) here is crucial. It doesn't mean close your eyes completely; it means lower your gaze from what is unlawful. The first look is accidental, the second is intentional. Shaytan doesn't work in the first glance; he works in the lingering stare. And immediately following this is وَيَحْفَظْنَ فُرُوجَهُنَّ—guard your private parts. The Quran consistently links the eyes to the heart, and the heart to action. What you allow your eyes to consume eventually dictates what your hands do, what your feet walk toward, and what your heart desires.

Then comes the famous legislation: وَلَا يُبْدِينَ زِينَتَهُنَّ إِلَّا مَا ظَهَرَ مِنْهَا—do not display your adornment except what naturally appears. Scholars differed on what "what appears" means, but the majority understood it as the face and hands, or what is practically unavoidable in daily movement. The real command follows: وَلْيَضْرِبْنَ بِخُمُرِهِنَّ عَلَىٰ جُيُوبِهِنَّ. The word خُمُر is the plural of خِمَار, which literally means a cover. In pre-Islamic Arabia, women wore headscarves, but they tied them behind the neck, leaving the chest and neckline exposed. Allah ﷻ commands: draw them forward over the جُيُوب—the chest, the neckline, the opening of the garment. This wasn't just about fabric; it was about dignity, about drawing a boundary between the private self and public space. The chest area is where emotion, vulnerability, and intimacy reside. Covering it wasn't repression; it was protection.

Then Allah ﷻ lists the exceptions—those before whom adornment may be shown. Notice the order: husbands, fathers, fathers-in-law, sons, sons-in-law, brothers, nephews... It's a comprehensive network of mahram relationships. But then it says: نِسَآئِهِنَّ (their women), مَا مَلَكَتْ أَيْمَـٰنُهُنَّ (those their right hands possess), or the male attendants lacking physical desire, and children not yet aware of women's private matters. This list teaches us something profound: modesty isn't isolation. Islam recognizes human social nature. You're not supposed to hide from society; you're supposed to interact with it within boundaries of dignity and mutual respect. The exceptions prove the rule: cover yourself from those outside your sacred circle, but relax within it.

Then comes a specific, almost cinematic detail: وَلَا يَضْرِبْنَ بِأَرْجُلِهِنَّ لِيُعْلَمَ مَا يُخْفِينَ مِن زِينَتِهِنَّ—"Do not stamp your feet to make known what you conceal of your adornment." In that era, some women wore ankle bracelets or chains that would jingle or catch the light when they walked. Intentionally drawing attention to hidden beauty becomes a form of subtle exhibitionism. Allah ﷻ addresses not just the outward covering, but the inward intention. Modesty isn't just about what you wear; it's about why you wear it. Are you dressing to be noticed, or dressing to be respected? The Quran targets the psychology behind the action.

The ayah concludes with a collective call: وَتُوبُوٓا۟ إِلَى ٱللَّهِ جَمِيعًا أَيُّهَ ٱلْمُؤْمِنُونَ لَعَلَّكُمْ تُفْلِحُونَ—"And turn to Allah in repentance, all of you, O believers, that you may succeed." Notice it's addressed to everyone, men and women. Why? Because modesty isn't a "women's issue." It's a communal responsibility. Men lower their gaze. Women lower their gaze. Society creates an environment of dignity. Success (تُفْلِحُونَ) is tied directly to this collective return to Allah. Repentance isn't just for sins; it's for realigning our cultural habits, our fashion choices, our social media presence, our public behavior with divine boundaries. When an entire community turns back to Allah with this consciousness, that's when real success begins.

translation: And marry the unmarried among you and the righteous among your male slaves and female slaves. If they should be poor, Allah will enrich them from His bounty, and Allah is all-Encompassing and Knowing
commentary:
وَأَنكِحُوا۟ ٱلْأَيَـٰمَىٰ مِنكُمْ وَٱلصَّـٰلِحِينَ مِنْ عِبَادِكُمْ وَإِمَآئِكُمْ ۚ إِن يَكُونُوا۟ فُقَرَآءَ يُغْنِهِمُ ٱللَّهُ مِن فَضْلِهِۦ ۗ وَٱللَّهُ وَٰسِعٌ عَلِيمٌ

Allah ﷻ commands the Muslim community to facilitate marriage for specific groups who may face particular challenges: the widows among them, and the righteous among their servants—both male (عَبْد) and female (أَمَة). The plural أَيَامَى refers specifically to widows, though by extension this includes divorced women and men who have struggled to find marriage.

This command addresses a critical social issue. Marriage services, especially for previously married individuals, hold significant importance in Islam. Yet in contemporary Muslim societies, particularly in the East, divorce carries an unfair stigma—as if it were a curse that renders someone untouchable. This contradicts the Islamic perspective entirely. Divorce, while regrettable, is neither haram nor does it indicate that either party is inherently evil. Sometimes two good people simply aren't compatible. Sometimes abuse necessitates separation. Whatever the circumstances, divorced individuals should not be ostracized or prevented from remarrying.

In an era when finding righteous spouses is increasingly difficult, families must not close this door. Communities should actively support remarriage for the previously married. Organizations have emerged to address this need, recognizing that mosques, while central to community life, cannot provide every specialized service. Just as cities should have endorsed counseling services independent of any single mosque's budget, they should support marriage facilitation services for those seeking to remarry.

The ayah concludes with a powerful reassurance about financial concerns: إِن يَكُونُوا۟ فُقَرَآءَ يُغْنِهِمُ ٱللَّهُ مِن فَضْلِهِۦ—"If they are poor, Allah will enrich them from His bounty." This directly addresses a common barrier to marriage. Allah ﷻ is وَٰسِعٌ (vast, all-encompassing) and عَلِيمٌ (All-Knowing)—He knows the circumstances and has the boundless means to provide.

translation: But let them who find not [the means for] marriage abstain [from sexual relations] until Allah enriches them from His bounty. And those who seek a contract [for eventual emancipation] from among whom your right hands possess - then make a contract with them if you know there is within them goodness and give them from the wealth of Allah which He has given you. And do not compel your slave girls to prostitution, if they desire chastity, to seek [thereby] the temporary interests of worldly life. And if someone should compel them, then indeed, Allah is [to them], after their compulsion, Forgiving and Merciful
commentary:
وَلْيَسْتَعْفِفِ ٱلَّذِينَ لَا يَجِدُونَ نِكَاحًا حَتَّىٰ يُغْنِيَهُمُ ٱللَّهُ مِن فَضْلِهِۦ ۗ وَٱلَّذِينَ يَبْتَغُونَ ٱلْكِتَـٰبَ مِمَّا مَلَكَتْ أَيْمَـٰنُكُمْ فَكَاتِبُوهُمْ إِنْ عَلِمْتُمْ فِيهِمْ خَيْرًا ۖ وَءَاتُوهُم مِّن مَّالِ ٱللَّهِ ٱلَّذِىٓ ءَاتَىٰكُمْ ۚ وَلَا تُكْرِهُوا۟ فَتَيَـٰتِكُمْ عَلَى ٱلْبِغَآءِ إِنْ أَرَدْنَ تَحَصُّنًا لِّتَبْتَغُوا۟ عَرَضَ ٱلْحَيَوٰةِ ٱلدُّنْيَا ۚ وَمَن يُكْرِههُّنَّ فَإِنَّ ٱللَّهَ مِنۢ بَعْدِ إِكْرَٰهِهِنَّ غَفُورٌ رَّحِيمٌ

For those who cannot find the means to marry, Allah ﷻ prescribes يَسْتَعْفِفِ —active self-restraint and protection. The intensity of this command acknowledges a reality: those struggling to find marriage face heightened vulnerability to temptation. Shaytaan specifically targets such individuals, so they must exercise extra vigilance—guarding their gaze, avoiding questionable situations and company, maintaining boundaries—until Allah enriches them from His grace with the opportunity to marry.

The ayah then addresses the institution of slavery as it existed at that time, commanding owners to grant freedom contracts (مُكَاتَبَة) to servants who seek them—provided there is خَيْر (good) in them. This condition refers to assurance that they won't become harmful to society upon release. Some individuals in pre-Islamic society, including slaves, engaged in criminal behavior that was overlooked due to their status.

The remarkable instruction that follows reveals Islam's merciful approach: وَءَاتُوهُم مِّن مَّالِ ٱللَّهِ—"Give them from Allah's wealth that He has given you." By describing wealth as belonging to Allah ﷻ, not the owner, the ayah encourages generosity in these freedom contracts. Perhaps don't even expect full financial compensation. Perhaps make the path to freedom easy: memorize some Quran, and here's some money to help you start your new life. It becomes an opportunity for the believer to earn tremendous reward.

The ayah then prohibits a vile practice that existed in both Mecca and Medina: وَلَا تُكْرِهُوا۟ فَتَيَـٰتِكُمْ عَلَى ٱلْبِغَآءِ—"Do not force your young women into prostitution." Owners used slave women to generate income through this filthy industry. If these women desire تَحَصُّن (dignity, protection, a decent household), they must not be coerced into prostitution for worldly gain.

This prohibition extends beyond the historical context. Wherever this exploitative industry exists—and it remains alive and thriving in various forms—Muslims must actively oppose it. It becomes a legitimate and necessary cause for the Muslim community to fight against.

The ayah's conclusion offers profound mercy: وَمَن يُكْرِههُّنَّ فَإِنَّ ٱللَّهَ مِنۢ بَعْدِ إِكْرَٰهِهِنَّ غَفُورٌ رَّحِيمٌ—"Whoever forces them, then indeed Allah, after their coercion, is Forgiving and Merciful." The forgiveness here is for the victims, not the perpetrators. Women forced into such circumstances, who later escape or are freed, should not carry guilt. Allah ﷻ knows they were coerced, and His forgiveness and mercy encompass them.

translation: And We have certainly sent down to you distinct verses and examples from those who passed on before you and an admonition for those who fear Allah
commentary:
وَلَقَدْ أَنزَلْنَآ إِلَيْكُمْ ءَايَـٰتٍ مُّبَيِّنَـٰتٍ وَمَثَلًا مِّنَ ٱلَّذِينَ خَلَوْا۟ مِن قَبْلِكُمْ وَمَوْعِظَةً لِّلْمُتَّقِينَ

This ayah serves as a transition, declaring that Allah ﷻ has sent down آيَاتٍ مُّبَيِّنَـٰتٍ—thoroughly clarifying miraculous signs—along with examples from previous nations and profound counsel for those who seek to protect themselves (المتقين). What follows is among the most sublime examples Allah ﷻ presents in the Quran.

translation: Allah is the Light of the heavens and the earth. The example of His light is like a niche within which is a lamp, the lamp is within glass, the glass as if it were a pearly [white] star lit from [the oil of] a blessed olive tree, neither of the east nor of the west, whose oil would almost glow even if untouched by fire. Light upon light. Allah guides to His light whom He wills. And Allah presents examples for the people, and Allah is Knowing of all things
commentary:
۞ ٱللَّهُ نُورُ ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ وَٱلْأَرْضِ ۚ مَثَلُ نُورِهِۦ كَمِشْكَوٰةٍ فِيهَا مِصْبَاحٌ ۖ ٱلْمِصْبَاحُ فِى زُجَاجَةٍ ۖ ٱلزُّجَاجَةُ كَأَنَّهَا كَوْكَبٌ دُرِّىٌّ يُوقَدُ مِن شَجَرَةٍ مُّبَـٰرَكَةٍ زَيْتُونَةٍ لَّا شَرْقِيَّةٍ وَلَا غَرْبِيَّةٍ يَكَادُ زَيْتُهَا يُضِىٓءُ وَلَوْ لَمْ تَمْسَسْهُ نَارٌ ۚ نُّورٌ عَلَىٰ نُورٍ ۗ يَهْدِى ٱللَّهُ لِنُورِهِۦ مَن يَشَآءُ ۚ وَيَضْرِبُ ٱللَّهُ ٱلْأَمْثَـٰلَ لِلنَّاسِ ۗ وَٱللَّهُ بِكُلِّ شَىْءٍ عَلِيمٌ

**Allah is the Light of the heavens and the earth.** What does this profound declaration mean? Light enables us to see things as they truly are. In darkness, a blanket might appear to be a monster. Light reveals reality. Without Allah's light—without the iman and faith He places in hearts—we might observe the universe but never understand its true nature or worth. Allah ﷻ is the means by which the heavens and earth can be comprehended for what they genuinely are.

Yet how can limited human beings grasp something of Allah's infinite reality? The ayah explains: مَثَلُ نُورِهِۦ كَمِشْكَوٰةٍ—"The example of His light is like a niche." Notice the distancing language: "example" (مَثَل) and "like" (ك)—a double removal, emphasizing that this parallel is a distant approximation. What human beings can conceptually handle falls infinitely short of Allah's reality.

The imagery unfolds layer by layer. A مِشْكَاة is an arched niche carved into a wall where homes once placed lamps. The curved interior reflected light throughout the room. فِيهَا مِصْبَاحٌ—within it is a مِصْبَاح , literally "a device that makes morning"—a lamp that simulates daylight.

This lamp sits within زُجَاجَة —clear glass. The glass itself كَأَنَّهَا كَوْكَبٌ دُرِّىٌّ—"is as though it is a brilliantly shining star." The word دُرِّي indicates excessive, intense brightness. When light strikes this pristine glass at certain angles, it creates a star-like effect, with rays emanating from one brilliant point, like a twinkling pearl.

The fuel comes from شَجَرَةٍ مُّبَـٰرَكَةٍ زَيْتُونَةٍ—a blessed olive tree. The word مُبَارَكَة  indicates something that produces more good than expected, exceeding normal bounds. But not just any olive tree—لَّا شَرْقِيَّةٍ وَلَا غَرْبِيَّةٍ—neither eastern nor western. This describes a tree standing alone, not in a forest. If positioned at the forest's eastern edge, only its western side receives full sunlight; at the western edge, only its eastern side benefits. But a solitary tree in an open space receives sunlight from sunrise to sunset, producing the purest, most refined oil—like the exceptional olive trees of Palestine and Syria.

This oil is so pure, so refined, that يَكَادُ زَيْتُهَا يُضِىٓءُ وَلَوْ لَمْ تَمْسَسْهُ نَارٌ—"its oil would almost glow even if no fire touched it." Like highly flammable gasoline that seems to chase after flame, this oil is ready to ignite instantly. It possesses its own luminosity even before combustion.

Then comes the culmination: نُّورٌ عَلَىٰ نُورٍ—**Light upon light.**

Now let's understand what this exquisite metaphor teaches us. The niche in the wall parallels the human ribcage—the same arched shape. The lamp within represents the heart. Surrounding that heart is clear glass—the فِطْرَة , the pure natural disposition every human is born with, free from bias or corruption.

Inside the heart resides something extraordinarily pure, neither eastern nor western in its orientation—the رُوح , the soul breathed into the fetus by the angel after 120 days. The ruh is a creature of light, just as Allah ﷻ describes Himself as نُور (Light). This ruh constitutes potential light—luminosity waiting to be activated, fuel prepared to ignite.

The glass surrounding this inner light remains pristine and transparent in its natural state, allowing what's inside to shine outward and external light to penetrate inward. This represents the profound connection between inner and outer reality.

But here's the crucial understanding: the human body has appetites—food, clothing, shelter, attraction—all deriving from our earthly nature. We are created from earth, so our physical needs can only be satisfied by things originating from earth. Similarly, the ruh, created from light, has its own hunger. When the soul starves, only light can satisfy it. No earthly pleasure can fill that spiritual void, that emptiness people describe when something essential is missing inside them.

The pure oil is ready, the lamp is prepared, but what activates it? The fire—and that fire is revelation, the Quran, the guidance from Allah ﷻ. When a person's inner light finally meets the light of divine revelation, that's نُّورٌ عَلَىٰ نُورٍ—the potential light within connecting with the revealed light without. The software has been installed; revelation is the activation key that makes it fully functional and legitimate.

But there's a critical condition for this union: the glass must remain clean. Every sin, every spiritual transgression, every act of greed, jealousy, anger, or disobedience stains that glass. Accumulate enough darkness, and two catastrophes occur: the goodness trapped inside suffocates, unable to shine forth, and the light of revelation outside cannot penetrate the filth to reach the heart. It hits the stained glass and deflects, again and again.

Consider this reality: the Quraysh of Mecca knew Arabic better than we ever will. They witnessed the Quran's revelation firsthand, heard the Prophet's ﷺ character described in living detail, saw his noble conduct with their own eyes. They experienced the Quran's immediate context better than any generation since. Yet many still rejected it. Why? Intellectually, perhaps they recognized its truth. But spiritually, layers of filth coated their hearts. Light simply couldn't penetrate.

This is why someone with limited Arabic might read the Quran and suddenly exclaim, "I feel this! It's speaking directly to me! The Quran didn't read me—the Quran read me!" Their glass remained clean enough for the lights to meet.

At the end of the day, decisions aren't made purely by intellect. Islam teaches that hearts make the ultimate choice. A woman might evaluate a marriage proposal and find nothing objectively wrong—good family, good salary, compatible backgrounds—but say, "My heart says no." We recognize this truth: the heart leads our most important decisions.

This is why Allah ﷻ speaks of hearts tasting the sweetness of iman. That sensation must exist. For those who maintain inner purity somehow, who keep that glass clean through consciousness of Allah, when they encounter the Quran, something awakens inside. The dormant light recognizes its counterpart. نُّورٌ عَلَىٰ نُورٍ.

يَهْدِى ٱللَّهُ لِنُورِهِۦ مَن يَشَآءُ—"Allah guides to His light whom He wills." This sophisticated subject—how does someone come to faith?—Allah ﷻ makes accessible through this powerful example. Now when you see a lamp, remember faith. When you notice a dusty, dirty bulb barely giving light, reflect: what have I done to stain my heart?

A clean glass left untouched will naturally accumulate dust over time. Layers thicken if neglected. The parallel is clear: even without actively doing wrong, our hearts can become rusty. We must constantly work on spiritual cleansing through dua, avoiding things that harden the heart (excessive screen time, useless conversation, overindulgence in any appetite, becoming overly worldly), remembering Allah ﷻ, and engaging with His revelation.

This doesn't mean denying the world entirely—the Prophet ﷺ ate well, fasted, prayed, rested, wore good clothes, engaged fully with life. The warning is against overindulgence, which invariably has spiritual consequences. If you struggle to find tears in prayer, that's a sign: serious cleaning is needed.

وَيَضْرِبُ ٱللَّهُ ٱلْأَمْثَـٰلَ لِلنَّاسِ—"Allah strikes examples for people." These examples make complex realities accessible, memorable, transformative. Each time we encounter a lamp, we remember this profound truth about faith.

translation: [Such niches are] in mosques which Allah has ordered to be raised and that His name be mentioned therein; exalting Him within them in the morning and the evenings
commentary:
فِى بُيُوتٍ أَذِنَ ٱللَّهُ أَن تُرْفَعَ وَيُذْكَرَ فِيهَا ٱسْمُهُۥ يُسَبِّحُ لَهُۥ فِيهَا بِٱلْغُدُوِّ وَٱلْـَٔاصَالِ

The light imagery continues: these lamps burn فِى بُيُوتٍ أَذِنَ ٱللَّهُ أَن تُرْفَعَ—"in houses Allah has permitted to be raised." These are the mosques, but what does "raised" mean?

Literally, these should be prominent, elevated structures that people notice and look up to—not necessarily gaudy or excessive, but architecturally significant in their communities. Figuratively, and more importantly, they should be elevated above the influence of politics, greed, backbiting, jealousy, anger, and arguments. These houses are where people come specifically for light, so they must remain pure.

The human being is like the niche inside a house. Now Allah ﷻ expands the metaphor: the entire city functions like a body, and its chest cavity—its spiritual heart—is the mosque. It must be clean. It must be the place of purity, free from the very stains that dirty individual hearts.

وَيُذْكَرَ فِيهَا ٱسْمُهُۥ—His name is remembered within them. يُسَبِّحُ لَهُۥ فِيهَا بِٱلْغُدُوِّ وَٱلْـَٔاصَالِ—His perfection is declared within them in the mornings and evenings. The imagery ties together beautifully: creatures of light (angels) observe from the heavens. In the darkest hours of night, certain homes—not just mosques, but houses where believers pray—have their physical lights on. But angels see more than physical light; they perceive spiritual light. Some spiritual lamps burn bright in the darkness, niches illuminated, hearts engaged in worship.

translation: [Are] men whom neither commerce nor sale distracts from the remembrance of Allah and performance of prayer and giving of zakah. They fear a Day in which the hearts and eyes will [fearfully] turn about
commentary:
رِجَالٌ لَّا تُلْهِيهِمْ تِجَـٰرَةٌ وَلَا بَيْعٌ عَن ذِكْرِ ٱللَّهِ وَإِقَامِ ٱلصَّلَوٰةِ وَإِيتَآءِ ٱلزَّكَوٰةِ ۙ يَخَافُونَ يَوْمًا تَتَقَلَّبُ فِيهِ ٱلْقُلُوبُ وَٱلْأَبْصَـٰرُ

Who are these people whose lamps burn bright? رِجَالٌ—real men, great men. لَّا تُلْهِيهِمْ تِجَـٰرَةٌ—business does not distract them. وَلَا بَيْعٌ—not even sales.

This distinction is significant. Business encompasses many components: accounting, payroll, taxes, inventory, merchandising, warehousing. All necessary but often tedious. Yet what makes it worthwhile? The sale—that's the exciting part, the profitable moment, the reason for everything else. Someone fills their cart, about to purchase that item sitting on your shelf for months. The adhan calls. These men don't hesitate. The sale can wait.

They're busy with accounts, busy with payroll—yes, busy with necessary work. But when the call to prayer sounds, nothing holds them back. عَن ذِكْرِ ٱللَّهِ—from the remembrance of Allah, وَإِقَامِ ٱلصَّلَوٰةِ—and establishing prayer, وَإِيتَآءِ ٱلزَّكَوٰةِ—and giving zakah.

The contrast with contemporary reality is stark. In some Muslim lands, the adhan sounds, the volume on the music merely decreases, and business continues exactly as before. Nobody rises. Some don't even turn off inappropriate content playing in their shops—they just lower the volume temporarily, then raise it again when the adhan ends. Can there be any light in such hearts? Any نُّورٌ عَلَىٰ نُورٍ when those hearts stand before Allah ﷻ?

These righteous ones don't forget their obligations even during lucrative sales—they don't compromise their deen for temporary gain. يَخَافُونَ يَوْمًا تَتَقَلَّبُ فِيهِ ٱلْقُلُوبُ وَٱلْأَبْصَـٰرُ—"They fear a Day when hearts and eyes will turn over," will be rattled and shaken violently. The word تَتَقَلَّبُ suggests intense back-and-forth motion, like violently shaking a container to see what's inside.

On that Day, hearts will be shaken to reveal their contents. Is the glass clean or dirty? These believers fear that examination, so they work constantly to maintain purity, ensuring their hearts remain prepared for that inevitable inspection.

translation: That Allah may reward them [according to] the best of what they did and increase them from His bounty. And Allah gives provision to whom He wills without account
commentary:
لِيَجْزِيَهُمُ ٱللَّهُ أَحْسَنَ مَا عَمِلُوا۟ وَيَزِيدَهُم مِّن فَضْلِهِۦ ۗ وَٱللَّهُ يَرْزُقُ مَن يَشَآءُ بِغَيْرِ حِسَابٍ

Why do they maintain such devotion? لِيَجْزِيَهُمُ ٱللَّهُ أَحْسَنَ مَا عَمِلُوا۟—so that Allah may compensate them with the best of what they did. This is a beautiful, repeated promise in the Quran. Perhaps you prayed every day for years, but one Isha prayer stands out—you wept genuine tears, understood every word, begged Allah ﷻ for forgiveness in every prostration, forgot you were standing next to anyone else. Just you and Allah ﷻ. That kind of prayer might come once a year.

Allah ﷻ says He'll compensate based on your best performance. Like being graded on a curve where your highest score becomes your average—Allah takes your peak and makes that your standard. The best you achieved becomes the baseline for your reward.

وَيَزِيدَهُم مِّن فَضْلِهِۦ—and He will increase them from His favor. That was the best you could do, the limit of your capacity. But then Allah ﷻ adds even more, beyond what you earned, pure grace. How many times has His favor been mentioned in this surah? "Had it not been for Allah's favor... had it not been for His favor..." Now: I'll increase you from My favor.

وَٱللَّهُ يَرْزُقُ مَن يَشَآءُ بِغَيْرِ حِسَابٍ—"Allah provides for whom He wills without account." This appears immediately after mentioning those whom business and sales don't distract. The connection is profound: you worry about physical provision (rizq)? Allah ﷻ is the Provider. He gives every creature its sustenance—every bird, every worm, every ant receives rizq from Him. But He's offering you something greater: spiritual rizq. While providing your physical needs, He invites you to receive spiritual provision of a higher nature.

The increase He promises بِغَيْرِ حِسَابٍ—without measure, beyond counting, unquantifiable. Your bonus is so vast you cannot see its end. That is the mercy of Allah ﷻ.

translation: But those who disbelieved - their deeds are like a mirage in a lowland which a thirsty one thinks is water until, when he comes to it, he finds it is nothing but finds Allah before Him, and He will pay him in full his due; and Allah is swift in account
commentary:
وَٱلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوٓا۟ أَعْمَـٰلُهُمْ كَسَرَابٍۭ بِقِيعَةٍ يَحْسَبُهُ ٱلظَّمْـَٔانُ مَآءً حَتَّىٰٓ إِذَا جَآءَهُۥ لَمْ يَجِدْهُ شَيْـًٔا وَوَجَدَ ٱللَّهَ عِندَهُۥ فَوَفَّىٰهُ حِسَابَهُۥ ۗ وَٱللَّهُ سَرِيعُ ٱلْحِسَابِ

After describing the believers whose deeds earn unprecedented reward, Allah ﷻ presents a stark contrast: وَٱلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوٓا۟—those who disbelieved. This doesn't refer only to obviously evil people. Disbelievers can engage in charitable works, establish hospitals, perform humanitarian service, sometimes even fight for just causes alongside Muslims. They might be genuinely good people by worldly standards.

A question arises: will Allah ﷻ punish such individuals? After all those good deeds? The answer requires understanding what's missing. Yes, they possess that dormant light—every human has it. Yes, they may do good things. But the problem? Their inner light hasn't met the light of revelation. Some light exists but remains incomplete. As Allah ﷻ said earlier, He completes His light—completion occurs when inner potential meets outer revelation. For these individuals, that meeting never happened. Perhaps they died without caring for that light, convinced their good deeds sufficed, never pursuing revelation or genuinely considering it.

Allah ﷻ describes their deeds: أَعْمَـٰلُهُمْ كَسَرَابٍۭ بِقِيعَةٍ—their deeds are like a mirage in a desert plain. The word سَرَاب  refers to something appearing to be water but isn't quite—the shimmering illusion in barren land. يَحْسَبُهُ ٱلظَّمْـَٔانُ مَآءً—the desperately thirsty person assumes it's water.

Picture someone dying of dehydration, certain they see water ahead. They drag themselves forward with their last strength. حَتَّىٰٓ إِذَا جَآءَهُۥ لَمْ يَجِدْهُ شَيْـًٔا—until when he reaches it, he finds nothing there. The crushing disappointment of that moment—expecting salvation, finding emptiness.

وَوَجَدَ ٱللَّهَ عِندَهُۥ—but he found Allah there instead. The One he never believed in, the One he never acknowledged, he finds Him there. فَوَفَّىٰهُ حِسَابَهُۥ—then He fulfills his account, gives him exactly what he deserves.

Notice the contrast with the previous ayah. The believers received rewards beyond measure, بِغَيْرِ حِسَابٍ—without accounting. This person receives precisely what he deserves—exact, calculated recompense. If anyone on Judgment Day gets exactly what they deserve with no mercy, that means punishment. That means hellfire. Our deeds alone don't earn Paradise. Allah ﷻ must forgive, must show mercy, must bury our sins for us to enter. That's not something we deserve; that's pure grace given on top of everything.

وَٱللَّهُ سَرِيعُ ٱلْحِسَابِ—and Allah is swift in account.

translation: Or [they are] like darknesses within an unfathomable sea which is covered by waves, upon which are waves, over which are clouds - darknesses, some of them upon others. When one puts out his hand [therein], he can hardly see it. And he to whom Allah has not granted light - for him there is no light
commentary:
أَوْ كَظُلُمَـٰتٍ فِى بَحْرٍ لُّجِّىٍّ يَغْشَىٰهُ مَوْجٌ مِّن فَوْقِهِۦ مَوْجٌ مِّن فَوْقِهِۦ سَحَابٌ ۚ ظُلُمَـٰتٌۢ بَعْضُهَا فَوْقَ بَعْضٍ إِذَآ أَخْرَجَ يَدَهُۥ لَمْ يَكَدْ يَرَىٰهَا ۗ وَمَن لَّمْ يَجْعَلِ ٱللَّهُ لَهُۥ نُورًا فَمَا لَهُۥ مِن نُّورٍ

The previous ayah described disbelievers who performed some good deeds—their works amounted to a mirage. Now comes a third category, the opposite of نُّورٌ عَلَىٰ نُورٍ (light upon light): ظُلُمَـٰتٌۢ بَعْضُهَا فَوْقَ بَعْضٍ (darkness upon darkness). This describes not merely disbelievers, but those who combine disbelief with active evil—vicious criminals, cruel oppressors, people of profound wickedness.

أَوْ كَظُلُمَـٰتٍ فِى بَحْرٍ لُّجِّىٍّ—or like layers of darkness in a deep, insistent ocean. لُجِّىّ describes an ocean that persists endlessly—you look for land, see only water, look again moments later, still only water. The horizon offers no hope, no end to the vast expanse. Someone stranded at sea searches desperately: is there land? No. Now? No. The ocean continues relentlessly.

يَغْشَىٰهُ مَوْجٌ—a wave covers him, crashes over him. مِّن فَوْقِهِۦ مَوْجٌ—then another wave on top of that. He barely recovers from the first when the second slams down, forcing him underwater. Already there's darkness (nighttime, the deep ocean). Add the drowning terror of wave after wave.

مِّن فَوْقِهِۦ سَحَابٌ—and above him are heavy clouds. Even if he hoped for moonlight to guide him, those clouds block it completely. ظُلُمَـٰتٌۢ بَعْضُهَا فَوْقَ بَعْضٍ—layers of darkness, one piled upon another.

إِذَآ أَخْرَجَ يَدَهُۥ لَمْ يَكَدْ يَرَىٰهَا—when he holds out his hand, he can almost not even see it. This powerful figure of speech describes more than physical blindness. He almost doesn't realize when he commits evil deeds. He has no sense that his actions are wrong. He doesn't flinch. 

Consider murderers and hardened criminals—do they even feel guilt? They can't almost can't even see what they're doing. It's an out-of-body experience. They don't care, don't recognize the gravity of their actions. Multiple layers of darkness have accumulated: rejection of Allah ﷻ, rejection of revelation, active pursuit of evil, hardened hearts impervious to remorse.

وَمَن لَّمْ يَجْعَلِ ٱللَّهُ لَهُۥ نُورًا فَمَا لَهُۥ مِن نُّورٍ—"For whoever Allah has not made light, there is no light for him." If Allah ﷻ Himself doesn't grant someone light from revelation, if He doesn't illuminate their path, then no light exists for them whatsoever. No amount of worldly success, intellectual achievement, or even outward good deeds can substitute for the light that comes only from Allah ﷻ.

translation: Do you not see that Allah is exalted by whomever is within the heavens and the earth and [by] the birds with wings spread [in flight]? Each [of them] has known his [means of] prayer and exalting [Him], and Allah is Knowing of what they do
commentary:
أَلَمْ تَرَ أَنَّ ٱللَّهَ يُسَبِّحُ لَهُۥ مَن فِى ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ وَٱلْأَرْضِ وَٱلطَّيْرُ صَـٰٓفَّـٰتٍ ۖ كُلٌّ قَدْ عَلِمَ صَلَاتَهُۥ وَتَسْبِيحَهُۥ ۗ وَٱللَّهُ عَلِيمٌۢ بِمَا يَفْعَلُونَ

أَلَمْ تَرَ—don't you see? Haven't you observed? أَنَّ ٱللَّهَ يُسَبِّحُ لَهُۥ مَن فِى ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ وَٱلْأَرْضِ—that everything in the heavens and earth declares Allah's perfection. وَٱلطَّيْرُ صَـٰٓفَّـٰتٍ—and the birds as they spread their wings. The word صَـٰٓفَّـٰت describes birds gliding, not flapping—swimming through the air. This swimming imagery connects to تَسْبِيح, which literally means swimming but refers to declaring Allah's glory.

كُلٌّ قَدْ عَلِمَ صَلَاتَهُۥ وَتَسْبِيحَهُۥ—each one knows its own salah and its own tasbih. Birds lined up on a wire suddenly take flight together—that's their iqamah, their call to their particular form of prayer. In the morning, their chirping constitutes their tasbih. They know how to perform their worship.

The question becomes: do you know your salah? Do you know your tasbih? Because the birds certainly know theirs.

An important linguistic distinction: this worship happens as فِطْرَة (unconscious, natural function) like breathing or seeing—you don't consciously think about it, it simply occurs. Animals and creatures don't have conscious intention the way humans do. But for us, worship shouldn't be mere fitrah, unconscious routine. Our tasbih should be intentional—'amal (conscious action), not reduced to mindless repetition while our attention wanders elsewhere.

وَٱللَّهُ عَلِيمٌۢ بِمَا يَفْعَلُونَ—and Allah has full knowledge of what they do.

translation: And to Allah belongs the dominion of the heavens and the earth, and to Allah is the destination
commentary:
وَلِلَّهِ مُلْكُ ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ وَٱلْأَرْضِ ۖ وَإِلَى ٱللَّهِ ٱلْمَصِيرُ

وَلِلَّهِ مُلْكُ ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ وَٱلْأَرْضِ—to Allah alone belongs the kingdom, the dominion, the sovereignty of the heavens and the earth. Everything in creation ultimately belongs to Him.

وَإِلَى ٱللَّهِ ٱلْمَصِيرُ—and to Allah alone is the final return. Everyone and everything returns to Him. This declaration reminds us that all ownership, all power, all authority rests with Allah ﷻ alone. Whatever we possess, whatever position we hold, whatever influence we wield—all of it belongs to Him, and we're returning to Him to give account.

translation: Do you not see that Allah drives clouds? Then He brings them together, then He makes them into a mass, and you see the rain emerge from within it. And He sends down from the sky, mountains [of clouds] within which is hail, and He strikes with it whom He wills and averts it from whom He wills. The flash of its lightening almost takes away the eyesight
commentary:
أَلَمْ تَرَ أَنَّ ٱللَّهَ يُزْجِى سَحَابًا ثُمَّ يُؤَلِّفُ بَيْنَهُۥ ثُمَّ يَجْعَلُهُۥ رُكَامًا فَتَرَى ٱلْوَدْقَ يَخْرُجُ مِنْ خِلَـٰلِهِۦ وَيُنَزِّلُ مِنَ ٱلسَّمَآءِ مِن جِبَالٍ فِيهَا مِنۢ بَرَدٍ فَيُصِيبُ بِهِۦ مَن يَشَآءُ وَيَصْرِفُهُۥ عَن مَّن يَشَآءُ ۖ يَكَادُ سَنَا بَرْقِهِۦ يَذْهَبُ بِٱلْأَبْصَـٰرِ

Again, أَلَمْ تَرَ—don't you see? Haven't you observed? أَنَّ ٱللَّهَ يُزْجِى سَحَابًا—that Allah drives clouds gently forward. The word يُزْجِى suggests something being pushed along slowly, like nudging something that offers little resistance. Allah ﷻ pushes the clouds across the sky.

ثُمَّ يُؤَلِّفُ بَيْنَهُۥ—then He brings them together, causes union between them. Separate clouds merge, join together. ثُمَّ يَجْعَلُهُۥ رُكَامًا—then He makes them into layers, piled one upon another. We observe this far better now than in earlier times. From airplanes, we see clouds at our level, clouds above us, clouds below us—distinct layers stacked in the atmosphere.

فَتَرَى ٱلْوَدْقَ يَخْرُجُ مِنْ خِلَـٰلِهِۦ—then you see the rain emerging from between them. الْوَدْق describes light, steady rain that continues trickling down.

وَيُنَزِّلُ مِنَ ٱلسَّمَآءِ مِن جِبَالٍ فِيهَا مِنۢ بَرَدٍ—and He sends down from the sky, from mountains within it, hail. The description suggests hail so massive it seems like remnants of mountains falling from the sky—sometimes meteor showers, but often simply enormous hailstones that strike with devastating force.

فَيُصِيبُ بِهِۦ مَن يَشَآءُ—then He strikes with it whom He wills. Every piece of hail is a targeted bullet. That specific hailstone that hit your car, cracked your windshield in that precise way—Allah ﷻ targeted it. Some people die from hail strikes. That's divine sniper fire, each piece assigned its destination and impact by Allah's command.

وَيَصْرِفُهُۥ عَن مَّن يَشَآءُ—and He turns it away from whom He wills. A deadly piece of hail plummets toward you, but at the last instant Allah ﷻ diverts it. It lands centimeters away. You think, "Whoa, that was close!" In reality, you just escaped death by Allah's mercy. That hail had your name on it, then He redirected it at the final moment. But you have no idea—you just thought it was awesome.

يَكَادُ سَنَا بَرْقِهِۦ يَذْهَبُ بِٱلْأَبْصَـٰرِ—the flash of its lightning almost takes away the vision. سَنَا refers to the intense brightness, the flash so powerful it leaves temporary blind spots, dark patches around your eyes for moments afterward. Strong camera flashes or lightning can do this—for several seconds you're partially blinded, vision slowly returning.

These natural phenomena—clouds, rain, hail, lightning—demonstrate Allah's absolute control over creation. Each element moves according to His will, strikes or misses according to His decree. The same power that controls the universe controls every detail of our lives. Nothing happens randomly. Everything unfolds according to divine wisdom, whether we perceive it or not.

This series of ayahs moves from the profound metaphor of light meeting light in the human heart, through descriptions of believers and disbelievers, to culminating reminders of Allah's sovereignty over all creation. The message threads throughout: seek the light, maintain its purity, recognize who controls everything, and understand that all affairs return to Him alone.

translation: Allah alternates the night and the day. Indeed in that is a lesson for those who have vision
commentary:
يُقَلِّبُ ٱللَّهُ ٱلَّيْلَ وَٱلنَّهَارَ ۚ إِنَّ فِى ذَٰلِكَ لَعِبْرَةً لِّأُو۟لِى ٱلْأَبْصَـٰرِ

Allah ﷻ alternates the night and the day. The word يُقَلِّبُ  means to turn something over, to alternate, to change from one state to another. This alternation isn't just about time passing—it's about transformation. Day becomes night, night becomes day, in a constant rhythm established by the Creator.

The ayah concludes with a powerful statement: إِنَّ فِى ذَٰلِكَ لَعِبْرَةً لِّأُو۟لِى ٱلْأَبْصَـٰرِ—"Indeed, in that is a tremendous lesson for those who truly possess insight." The word عِبْرَة (ibrah) refers to a lesson that should make you stop and reflect, a warning that should penetrate deep into your consciousness. And this lesson is specifically for أُو۟لِى ٱلْأَبْصَـٰرِ—those who have basaar, real vision. This isn't just about physical sight; it's about the ability to see beyond the surface, to recognize the divine wisdom in the patterns of creation.

translation: Allah has created every [living] creature from water. And of them are those that move on their bellies, and of them are those that walk on two legs, and of them are those that walk on four. Allah creates what He wills. Indeed, Allah is over all things competent
commentary:
وَٱللَّهُ خَلَقَ كُلَّ دَآبَّةٍ مِّن مَّآءٍ ۖ فَمِنْهُم مَّن يَمْشِى عَلَىٰ بَطْنِهِۦ وَمِنْهُم مَّن يَمْشِى عَلَىٰ رِجْلَيْنِ وَمِنْهُم مَّن يَمْشِى عَلَىٰٓ أَرْبَعٍ ۚ يَخْلُقُ ٱللَّهُ مَا يَشَآءُ ۚ إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ عَلَىٰ كُلِّ شَىْءٍ قَدِيرٌ

Allah ﷻ created every creature from water. This fundamental truth connects all living beings—whether they crawl on their bellies like snakes, walk on two legs like humans, birds, and certain other creatures, or move on four legs like most animals. The diversity is staggering, yet the origin is unified: water, the essence of life itself.

The ayah concludes with two critical declarations. First: يَخْلُقُ ٱللَّهُ مَا يَشَآءُ—"Allah creates whatever He wants." There's no limit to His creative power. Then comes the comprehensive statement: إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ عَلَىٰ كُلِّ شَىْءٍ قَدِيرٌ—"Certainly Allah is in complete control over all things."

This is the ayah that should come to mind when you watch nature documentaries, when you observe the incredible variety of creatures that inhabit this earth. Every creature you see, from the smallest insect to the largest whale, is a testament to the creative power and wisdom of Allah ﷻ.

translation: We have certainly sent down distinct verses. And Allah guides whom He wills to a straight path
commentary:
لَّقَدْ أَنزَلْنَآ ءَايَـٰتٍ مُّبَيِّنَـٰتٍ ۚ وَٱللَّهُ يَهْدِى مَن يَشَآءُ إِلَىٰ صِرَٰطٍ مُّسْتَقِيمٍ

Allah ﷻ declares: لَّقَدْ أَنزَلْنَآ ءَايَـٰتٍ مُّبَيِّنَـٰتٍ—"We have certainly sent down ayat that are mubayinat"—clear, clarifying, miraculous signs that illuminate the truth. These ayat don't leave you in confusion; they bring clarity. They're not vague or ambiguous; they're meant to make things crystal clear.

But then comes an essential truth about guidance: وَٱللَّهُ يَهْدِى مَن يَشَآءُ إِلَىٰ صِرَٰطٍ مُّسْتَقِيمٍ—"And Allah Himself guides whoever wants to be guided to a straight path." The phrasing here is crucial. Allah ﷻ guides مَن يَشَآءُ —whoever wants it, whoever seeks it with sincerity. The signs are clear, but guidance requires a heart that's open and seeking. You can lead a horse to water, but you can't make it drink. Allah ﷻ provides the clear signs; whether someone benefits from them depends on their willingness to receive guidance.

translation: But the hypocrites say, "We have believed in Allah and in the Messenger, and we obey"; then a party of them turns away after that. And those are not believers
commentary:
وَيَقُولُونَ ءَامَنَّا بِٱللَّهِ وَبِٱلرَّسُولِ وَأَطَعْنَا ثُمَّ يَتَوَلَّىٰ فَرِيقٌ مِّنْهُم مِّنۢ بَعْدِ ذَٰلِكَ ۚ وَمَآ أُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ بِٱلْمُؤْمِنِينَ

Now the surah shifts to address hypocrisy directly. These are the words of the hypocrites in Medina who would come to the Prophet ﷺ and say, "We believe in Allah and the Messenger, and we obey." Their words sound beautiful—a complete declaration of faith and commitment.

But then comes the reality: ثُمَّ يَتَوَلَّىٰ فَرِيقٌ مِّنْهُم—"Then a group of them turns away." After making these grand declarations, after swearing their loyalty, they turn their backs. They disappear when it's time to actually demonstrate that belief through action.

The conclusion is devastating: وَمَآ أُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ بِٱلْمُؤْمِنِينَ—"And those are not believers at all." Notice the structure here. In Arabic, you would normally say مَا هُمْ مِنَ الْمُؤْمِنِينَ (they are not from among the believers), but Allah ﷻ says بِٱلْمُؤْمِنِينَ—they are nothing like the believers. There's a group called "the believers"—the Sahaba (RA)—and these hypocrites are nothing like them. The comparison highlights just how far removed hypocrisy is from true faith.

translation: And when they are called to [the words of] Allah and His Messenger to judge between them, at once a party of them turns aside [in refusal]
commentary:
وَإِذَا دُعُوٓا۟ إِلَى ٱللَّهِ وَرَسُولِهِۦ لِيَحْكُمَ بَيْنَهُمْ إِذَا فَرِيقٌ مِّنْهُم مُّعْرِضُونَ

The pattern of hypocrisy continues. When they're called to Allah ﷻ and His Messenger ﷺ so that he may judge between them in their disputes, what happens? إِذَا فَرِيقٌ مِّنْهُم مُّعْرِضُونَ—"All of a sudden, there's a group among them that tries to ignore the call."

They start making excuses. "I didn't hear about the meeting." "I didn't realize it was for me." "Did the Messenger personally come to my house and invite me? No? Then how was I supposed to know?" These are lame excuses, and Allah ﷻ is exposing them for what they are. When the Prophet ﷺ calls Muslims to a gathering, to a judgment, to a battle, you don't get to say you weren't personally invited. A general doesn't knock on every soldier's door. When the call goes out, you answer.

translation: But if the right is theirs, they come to him in prompt obedience
commentary:
وَإِن يَكُن لَّهُمُ ٱلْحَقُّ يَأْتُوٓا۟ إِلَيْهِ مُذْعِنِينَ

Here's where the hypocrisy becomes crystal clear. وَإِن يَكُن لَّهُمُ ٱلْحَقُّ—"But if the truth is on their side"—if they know going into a situation that they're going to win the case, that the verdict will be in their favor, then what happens?

يَأْتُوٓا۟ إِلَيْهِ مُذْعِنِينَ—"They come to him like obedient camels with their heads lowered." The word مُذْعِنِينَ  comes from ذعن , which means to obey at the slightest hint, to be extremely compliant. It's like when you just point at your child and they immediately start doing their homework—that level of ready obedience.

Suddenly these hypocrites are model Muslims. "Let's go to the Prophet ﷺ! He's such a fair judge! This will make me look sincere, and I'm guaranteed to win!" But if the case might go against them? Nowhere to be found. This selective obedience reveals the truth: they're not committed to justice or to following the Messenger ﷺ. They're committed only to their own interests.

translation: Is there disease in their hearts? Or have they doubted? Or do they fear that Allah will be unjust to them, or His Messenger? Rather, it is they who are the wrongdoers
commentary:
أَفِى قُلُوبِهِم مَّرَضٌ أَمِ ٱرْتَابُوٓا۟ أَمْ يَخَافُونَ أَن يَحِيفَ ٱللَّهُ عَلَيْهِمْ وَرَسُولُهُۥ ۚ بَلْ أُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ هُمُ ٱلظَّـٰلِمُونَ

Allah ﷻ now asks rhetorical questions that cut to the heart of the matter. أَفِى قُلُوبِهِم مَّرَضٌ—"Is there a disease in their hearts?" Is that the problem? Is something fundamentally wrong with their spiritual condition?

أَمِ ٱرْتَابُوٓا۟—"Or have they fallen into doubt?" Are they doubting the truth of Islam, doubting the Prophet ﷺ, doubting Allah's guidance?

أَمْ يَخَافُونَ أَن يَحِيفَ ٱللَّهُ عَلَيْهِمْ وَرَسُولُهُۥ—"Or are they afraid that Allah and His Messenger will be biased against them?" The word يَحِيفَ  comes from حيف , which means to lean unfairly, to be biased. Are they actually worried that Allah ﷻ—who is the Most Just—and His Messenger ﷺ—who is known for his fairness—will somehow treat them unjustly? That Allah ﷻ will hold a grudge against them for their past actions?

The answer comes swiftly: بَلْ أُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ هُمُ ٱلظَّـٰلِمُونَ—"No! They are the ones who do wrong." Allah ﷻ and His Messenger ﷺ shouldn't be accused of wrongdoing. The problem isn't with divine justice; the problem is with their own corruption.

translation: The only statement of the [true] believers when they are called to Allah and His Messenger to judge between them is that they say, "We hear and we obey." And those are the successful
commentary:
إِنَّمَا كَانَ قَوْلَ ٱلْمُؤْمِنِينَ إِذَا دُعُوٓا۟ إِلَى ٱللَّهِ وَرَسُولِهِۦ لِيَحْكُمَ بَيْنَهُمْ أَن يَقُولُوا۟ سَمِعْنَا وَأَطَعْنَا ۚ وَأُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ هُمُ ٱلْمُفْلِحُونَ

After exposing the hypocrites, Allah ﷻ now shows us what true faith looks like. إِنَّمَا كَانَ قَوْلَ ٱلْمُؤْمِنِينَ—"The only statement of the believers"—when they're called to Allah ﷻ and His Messenger ﷺ to judge between them, whether they're guilty or innocent, whether the verdict will favor them or not—أَن يَقُولُوا۟ سَمِعْنَا وَأَطَعْنَا—they simply say, "We hear and we obey."

No excuses. No conditions. No "but what if?" Just سَمِعْنَا وَأَطَعْنَا—"We hear and we obey." This is one of the most beautiful, comprehensive phrases in the Quran. It captures the entire attitude of a believer: immediate, unconditional submission to Allah's command and the Prophet's guidance.

And the result? وَأُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ هُمُ ٱلْمُفْلِحُونَ—"Those are the ones who are ultimately successful." Real success, eternal success, doesn't come from clever maneuvering or selective obedience. It comes from wholehearted submission.

translation: And whoever obeys Allah and His Messenger and fears Allah and is conscious of Him - it is those who are the attainers
commentary:
وَمَن يُطِعِ ٱللَّهَ وَرَسُولَهُۥ وَيَخْشَ ٱللَّهَ وَيَتَّقْهِ فَأُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ هُمُ ٱلْفَآئِزُونَ

This ayah deserves careful attention because of one unusual grammatical feature. The standard form would be وَيَتَّقِهِ  with a kasrah (the "i" sound), but here it's وَيَتَّقْهِ  with a sukoon. In Arabic poetry and in the Quran for stylistic emphasis, this unusual form appears when the action itself is being highlighted intensely.

So when Allah ﷻ says وَيَخْشَ ٱللَّهَ وَيَتَّقْهِ, He's saying: "And truly fears Allah and goes out of his way to have taqwa of Him." The وَيَخْشَ relates to what has already happened—fearing Allah ﷻ over past mistakes, seeking forgiveness. The وَيَتَّقْهِ relates to the future—being extremely cautious, protective, vigilant about not falling into sin again.

This is significant because taqwa is mentioned throughout the Quran, but here Allah ﷻ uses this special emphasis. Ibn Abbas (RA) beautifully explained this ayah: Whoever obeys Allah (the mandatory commands) and obeys His Messenger ﷺ (the Sunnah), and fears Allah over what happened in the past, and has intense taqwa about what's coming in the future—those are the ones who win.

There's a story that captures the comprehensive nature of this ayah. A king once asked for one ayah that contained all of Islam, and this ayah was recited to him. It has everything: obey Allah, obey the Messenger, seek forgiveness for your past, protect yourself in your future. If you do this, you're successful. The entire spectrum of Islam is captured here.

translation: And they swear by Allah their strongest oaths that if you ordered them, they would go forth [in Allah 's cause]. Say, "Do not swear. [Such] obedience is known. Indeed, Allah is Acquainted with that which you do
commentary:
۞ وَأَقْسَمُوا۟ بِٱللَّهِ جَهْدَ أَيْمَـٰنِهِمْ لَئِنْ أَمَرْتَهُمْ لَيَخْرُجُنَّ ۖ قُل لَّا تُقْسِمُوا۟ ۖ طَاعَةٌ مَّعْرُوفَةٌ ۚ إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ خَبِيرٌۢ بِمَا تَعْمَلُونَ

The hypocrites are at it again. وَأَقْسَمُوا۟ بِٱللَّهِ جَهْدَ أَيْمَـٰنِهِمْ—"They swore by Allah, going out of their way in their oaths." Imagine them raising their right hands dramatically: "By Allah! If you command us to go to battle, we will absolutely come out!"

But when the Prophet ﷺ actually called them to battle, they didn't show up. Then they came back with excuses: "Oh, you never personally came to my house and told me to come. I didn't realize the call was for me specifically."

Allah's response to the Prophet ﷺ: قُل لَّا تُقْسِمُوا۟—"Tell them: Don't swear to Me. Please, don't waste My time with your oaths." طَاعَةٌ مَّعْرُوفَةٌ—"There's a general, known kind of obedience." When the Messenger ﷺ makes a general call, everyone understands they're being addressed. You don't need a personal invitation to your doorstep. This is an army; the general doesn't knock on every soldier's door. The call goes out, and you respond.

إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ خَبِيرٌۢ بِمَا تَعْمَلُونَ—"Allah knows exactly what you're up to." These lame excuses won't fly. Allah ﷻ sees through the performance, through the dramatic oaths, to the reality of their hearts.

translation: Say, "Obey Allah and obey the Messenger; but if you turn away - then upon him is only that [duty] with which he has been charged, and upon you is that with which you have been charged. And if you obey him, you will be [rightly] guided. And there is not upon the Messenger except the [responsibility for] clear notification
commentary:
قُلْ أَطِيعُوا۟ ٱللَّهَ وَأَطِيعُوا۟ ٱلرَّسُولَ ۖ فَإِن تَوَلَّوْا۟ فَإِنَّمَا عَلَيْهِ مَا حُمِّلَ وَعَلَيْكُم مَّا حُمِّلْتُمْ ۖ وَإِن تُطِيعُوهُ تَهْتَدُوا۟ ۚ وَمَا عَلَى ٱلرَّسُولِ إِلَّا ٱلْبَلَـٰغُ ٱلْمُبِينُ

The command is clear and direct: قُلْ أَطِيعُوا۟ ٱللَّهَ وَأَطِيعُوا۟ ٱلرَّسُولَ—"Say: Obey Allah and obey the Messenger." No ambiguity, no room for interpretation.

فَإِن تَوَلَّوْا۟—"But if you still turn away"—then understand this division of responsibility: فَإِنَّمَا عَلَيْهِ مَا حُمِّلَ—"He is only responsible for what he was charged with." The Prophet ﷺ has his job: to deliver the message clearly. وَعَلَيْكُم مَّا حُمِّلْتُمْ—"And you are only responsible for what you've been charged with"—which is to listen and obey.

وَإِن تُطِيعُوهُ تَهْتَدُوا۟—"And if you obey him, you will be guided." The path to guidance isn't mysterious or complicated. Obey the Messenger ﷺ, and guidance will follow.

وَمَا عَلَى ٱلرَّسُولِ إِلَّا ٱلْبَلَـٰغُ ٱلْمُبِينُ—"And the Messenger's only responsibility is to clearly communicate the message." Get this through your heads: the Prophet ﷺ is not a dictator. He's not going to force you to obey. He's not going to chase you down and make you submit. His job is البلاغ المبين—clear communication. The message itself, in its beauty and clarity, should be enough to convince you that he's worth following.

translation: Allah has promised those who have believed among you and done righteous deeds that He will surely grant them succession [to authority] upon the earth just as He granted it to those before them and that He will surely establish for them [therein] their religion which He has preferred for them and that He will surely substitute for them, after their fear, security, [for] they worship Me, not associating anything with Me. But whoever disbelieves after that - then those are the defiantly disobedient
commentary:
وَعَدَ ٱللَّهُ ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ مِنكُمْ وَعَمِلُوا۟ ٱلصَّـٰلِحَـٰتِ لَيَسْتَخْلِفَنَّهُمْ فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ كَمَا ٱسْتَخْلَفَ ٱلَّذِينَ مِن قَبْلِهِمْ وَلَيُمَكِّنَنَّ لَهُمْ دِينَهُمُ ٱلَّذِى ٱرْتَضَىٰ لَهُمْ وَلَيُبَدِّلَنَّهُم مِّنۢ بَعْدِ خَوْفِهِمْ أَمْنًا ۚ يَعْبُدُونَنِى لَا يُشْرِكُونَ بِى شَيْـًٔا ۚ وَمَن كَفَرَ بَعْدَ ذَٰلِكَ فَأُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ هُمُ ٱلْفَـٰسِقُونَ

This is a promise of victory, delivered at a time when victory seemed impossible. Allah ﷻ promises those who believe and do righteous deeds: لَيَسْتَخْلِفَنَّهُمْ فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ—"He will certainly establish them as successors in the land." The word استخلاف means to leave someone in charge, to give them authority and responsibility. It's the concept of khilafah—generations after generations being left in charge to steward the earth.

This promise comes كَمَا ٱسْتَخْلَفَ ٱلَّذِينَ مِن قَبْلِهِمْ—"just as He established those before them"—referring to previous believing communities who were granted authority after periods of hardship.

وَلَيُمَكِّنَنَّ لَهُمْ دِينَهُمُ—"And He will certainly fortify and stabilize their religion for them"—the religion that He is pleased with for them, which is Islam. The word تمكين means to make something stable, solid, unshakeable.

Now, here's what's remarkable: this promise is being made to Muslims who don't yet have stability. This surah came down either shortly before or after the Battle of Ahzab, when Medina was completely surrounded by enemy forces. The entire Muslim population was close to being wiped out. On top of that, there were Jewish tribes violating treaties, hypocrites making secret alliances with the Makkans, and people like Abdullah bin Ubayy working tirelessly to destroy Muslim morale from within.

The last thing Muslims had at this moment was stability. They were very much a work in progress. Yet Allah ﷻ is promising them: You will have authority in the land. This teaches us something critical—Medina, as it's often called in history books, wasn't yet a fully established Islamic state. It was the formative stages of an Islamic state. It was the egg, not yet the chick. There was no martial law, no formal consequences for abandoning the battlefield (300 people walked away at Uhud and faced no punishment), no formal judicial system for dealing with hypocrites who attacked the faith itself.

The stability came later, after Makkah was conquered. The dominoes started falling after the Treaty of Hudaybiyah, as we'll see when we reach Surah Al-Fath insha'Allah. But at this moment, stability is still a promise, not a reality.

وَلَيُبَدِّلَنَّهُم مِّنۢ بَعْدِ خَوْفِهِمْ أَمْنًا—"And He will certainly replace their fear with security." When Surah Al-Ahzab was revealed and the believers saw that intense state of fear with the city surrounded, they said, "This is what Allah and His Messenger promised us!" They recognized the fear as part of the divine plan, knowing that security would follow. They saw the hardship and said, "We know what's coming next." That's the power of having certainty in Allah's promise.

يَعْبُدُونَنِى لَا يُشْرِكُونَ بِى شَيْـًٔا—"They will worship Me alone, not associating any partners with Me." The society being promised is one built on pure tawheed, undiluted worship of Allah ﷻ alone.

وَمَن كَفَرَ بَعْدَ ذَٰلِكَ فَأُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ هُمُ ٱلْفَـٰسِقُونَ—"And whoever disbelieves after all of that, then those are the ones who are inherently corrupt." This is significant: people left Islam even during the Prophet's time ﷺ. But when an entire society has been established, when its justice, beauty, and perfection can be witnessed by everyone, when its benefits are clear for Muslims and non-Muslims alike—then when someone says, "I don't think this is the right religion" and walks away, they're considered a traitor to everything good they've witnessed. That's when such a person faces consequences in an established Islamic state.

translation: And establish prayer and give zakah and obey the Messenger - that you may receive mercy
commentary:
وَأَقِيمُوا۟ ٱلصَّلَوٰةَ وَءَاتُوا۟ ٱلزَّكَوٰةَ وَأَطِيعُوا۟ ٱلرَّسُولَ لَعَلَّكُمْ تُرْحَمُونَ

The Muslims are being promised victory, and they're thinking, "Yes! Victory is coming! What should we do? How do we prepare for this?"

Allah's response is beautifully simple: وَأَقِيمُوا۟ ٱلصَّلَوٰةَ—"Establish the prayer." Not just pray, but أقيموا—establish it, make it stand upright, perfect it, maintain it consistently.

وَءَاتُوا۟ ٱلزَّكَوٰةَ—"Give the zakat." Take care of the poor, purify your wealth, ensure social justice.

وَأَطِيعُوا۟ ٱلرَّسُولَ—"And obey the Messenger." The Messenger ﷺ is your guide to victory. Allah ﷻ will guide him, and he will guide you. If you falter in your loyalty, in your obedience, in your discipline behind the Messenger ﷺ, you won't qualify for what comes next.

لَعَلَّكُمْ تُرْحَمُونَ—"So that you may be shown mercy." Victory itself is an act of mercy. But mercy requires that you take the means seriously: prayer, charity, and complete obedience to the Prophet's guidance ﷺ.

translation: Never think that the disbelievers are causing failure [to Allah] upon the earth. Their refuge will be the Fire - and how wretched the destination
commentary:
لَا تَحْسَبَنَّ ٱلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا۟ مُعْجِزِينَ فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ ۚ وَمَأْوَىٰهُمُ ٱلنَّارُ ۖ وَلَبِئْسَ ٱلْمَصِيرُ

In the middle of all these challenges—with Makkans plotting, battles looming, and enemies surrounding—Allah ﷻ tells the believers: لَا تَحْسَبَنَّ ٱلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا۟ مُعْجِزِينَ فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ—"Don't you ever dare think that those who disbelieve are going to overpower anyone in the land."

Don't let that thought cross your mind. Even if the entire city is surrounded, even if the odds look impossible, don't you dare think they have the upper hand. They don't. Allah ﷻ has already decreed their fate: وَمَأْوَىٰهُمُ ٱلنَّارُ—"Their place of refuge is the Fire." وَلَبِئْسَ ٱلْمَصِيرُ—"And what a horrible place to return to."

This is psychological preparation for believers. In moments when the enemy seems overwhelming, when fear grips the heart, remember this ayah. They're not going to win.

translation: O you who have believed, let those whom your right hands possess and those who have not [yet] reached puberty among you ask permission of you [before entering] at three times: before the dawn prayer and when you put aside your clothing [for rest] at noon and after the night prayer. [These are] three times of privacy for you. There is no blame upon you nor upon them beyond these [periods], for they continually circulate among you - some of you, among others. Thus does Allah make clear to you the verses; and Allah is Knowing and Wise
commentary:
يَـٰٓأَيُّهَا ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ لِيَسْتَـْٔذِنكُمُ ٱلَّذِينَ مَلَكَتْ أَيْمَـٰنُكُمْ وَٱلَّذِينَ لَمْ يَبْلُغُوا۟ ٱلْحُلُمَ مِنكُمْ ثَلَـٰثَ مَرَّٰتٍ ۚ مِّن قَبْلِ صَلَوٰةِ ٱلْفَجْرِ وَحِينَ تَضَعُونَ ثِيَابَكُم مِّنَ ٱلظَّهِيرَةِ وَمِنۢ بَعْدِ صَلَوٰةِ ٱلْعِشَآءِ ۚ ثَلَـٰثُ عَوْرَٰتٍ لَّكُمْ ۚ لَيْسَ عَلَيْكُمْ وَلَا عَلَيْهِمْ جُنَاحٌۢ بَعْدَهُنَّ ۚ طَوَّٰفُونَ عَلَيْكُم بَعْضُكُمْ عَلَىٰ بَعْضٍ ۚ كَذَٰلِكَ يُبَيِّنُ ٱللَّهُ لَكُمُ ٱلْـَٔايَـٰتِ ۗ وَٱللَّهُ عَلِيمٌ حَكِيمٌ

In the middle of discussions about Makkans, battles, and the enemies surrounding Medina, Allah ﷻ suddenly addresses something entirely different: privacy within the home. This is the beauty of the Quran—it addresses the grand political landscape and the intimate details of family life with equal importance.

Those of you who have faith, make sure that الَّذِينَ مَلَكَتْ أَيْمَـٰنُكُمْ—the people you own (servants, slaves)—and وَٱلَّذِينَ لَمْ يَبْلُغُوا۟ ٱلْحُلُمَ—those who haven't reached maturity yet (children)—should ask your permission ثَلَـٰثَ مَرَّٰتٍ—three times.

When? مِّن قَبْلِ صَلَوٰةِ ٱلْفَجْرِ—before Fajr prayer. Picture this: you're asleep in your bedroom, and just before Fajr, your child walks in and climbs into bed between you and your spouse. Kids tend to kick the father and claim their spot: "Move over, Dad. This is mine." Allah ﷻ says: train your children that before they reach the age where they understand these matters, they should ask permission before entering your bedroom at this time.

وَحِينَ تَضَعُونَ ثِيَابَكُم مِّنَ ٱلظَّهِيرَةِ—"And when you take off your outer garments during the afternoon"—when you're taking an afternoon nap, changing clothes, or just resting. For many of us working full-time, this might not apply daily, but on weekends especially, this should be implemented. Close the door, tell the kids: "If you need something, knock first."

وَمِنۢ بَعْدِ صَلَوٰةِ ٱلْعِشَآءِ—"And after Isha prayer"—nighttime, when everyone should be going to sleep. But kids show up: "Can I have some ice cream?" They appear out of nowhere. At this time too, doors should be closed and they should knock.

These are called ثَلَـٰثُ عَوْرَٰتٍ لَّكُمْ—"three times of privacy for you." The word عورة refers to something that should remain hidden, private. These are the three windows of time when you really need privacy as parents.

Here's what's remarkable: Allah ﷻ knows human nature so well. Any parent reading this would say, "Oh man, yes! That's exactly when they show up!" It's so close to home. Allah ﷻ is telling us: these are the three times when you really don't want to see them, but they will absolutely show up. So be prepared, set boundaries, but don't be harsh about it.

لَيْسَ عَلَيْكُمْ وَلَا عَلَيْهِمْ جُنَاحٌۢ بَعْدَهُنَّ—"After those times, there's no harm on you or on them." The rest of the day, your home is an open space. Don't make your room off-limits all the time, because that creates curiosity: "What's going on in there? What are they hiding?" Let your kids play on your bed, run around, be comfortable. The home should feel open and welcoming—except during those three specific times.

طَوَّٰفُونَ عَلَيْكُم بَعْضُكُمْ عَلَىٰ بَعْضٍ—"They will be going back and forth around you, some of you upon others." The word طواف beautifully captures how children move—they go in circles, climb on you, run off, come back, climb on you again. You ask them, "Where are you going?" They don't know. They just move. Allah ﷻ captured this perfectly: طَوَّٰفُونَ—they're going to be coming around, hanging off you, then running off, then coming back.

كَذَٰلِكَ يُبَيِّنُ ٱللَّهُ لَكُمُ ٱلْـَٔايَـٰتِ—"That is how Allah clarifies the signs for you." وَٱللَّهُ عَلِيمٌ حَكِيمٌ—"And Allah is All-Knowing, All-Wise." He knows the dynamics of family life, the behavior of children, the needs of parents. This guidance isn't abstract; it's deeply practical.

Now, here's the beautiful part: when your child knocks on your door and asks, "Mama, can I come in?"—and you've taught them this because of this ayah—you get reward. They get reward. A simple act like knocking on a door becomes an act of worship because Allah ﷻ commanded it. Putting a small lock on the door so they have to knock? That becomes a means of implementing Quranic guidance. Your home becomes a place where Ibadah happens in the smallest, most intimate details.

translation: And when the children among you reach puberty, let them ask permission [at all times] as those before them have done. Thus does Allah make clear to you His verses; and Allah is Knowing and Wise
commentary:
وَإِذَا بَلَغَ ٱلْأَطْفَـٰلُ مِنكُمُ ٱلْحُلُمَ فَلْيَسْتَـْٔذِنُوا۟ كَمَا ٱسْتَـْٔذَنَ ٱلَّذِينَ مِن قَبْلِهِمْ ۚ كَذَٰلِكَ يُبَيِّنُ ٱللَّهُ لَكُمْ ءَايَـٰتِهِۦ ۗ وَٱللَّهُ عَلِيمٌ حَكِيمٌ

When children reach maturity—when they become teenagers, young adults—the rules change. فَلْيَسْتَـْٔذِنُوا۟ كَمَا ٱسْتَـْٔذَنَ ٱلَّذِينَ مِن قَبْلِهِمْ—"Then they should ask permission just like those mentioned before them"—meaning like adults, like outsiders who come to your home.

This is especially relevant if they've moved out. If your teenage or adult children are living independently now, they can't just walk into your home whenever they want, treating it like it's still their childhood home. They need to knock, ring the doorbell, ask permission like any other adult visitor. "But this is my mom's house!" No—the Quran says they should ask permission like everyone else.

Scholars have differed on whether this applies if they're still living under the same roof, but if they've moved out, the matter is clearer: they should treat visits to your home with the respect and boundaries that adult visitors observe.

كَذَٰلِكَ يُبَيِّنُ ٱللَّهُ لَكُمْ ءَايَـٰتِهِۦ—"That is how Allah clarifies His miraculous signs for you." وَٱللَّهُ عَلِيمٌ حَكِيمٌ—"And Allah is All-Knowing, All-Wise."

translation: And women of post-menstrual age who have no desire for marriage - there is no blame upon them for putting aside their outer garments [but] not displaying adornment. But to modestly refrain [from that] is better for them. And Allah is Hearing and Knowing
commentary:
وَٱلْقَوَٰعِدُ مِنَ ٱلنِّسَآءِ ٱلَّـٰتِى لَا يَرْجُونَ نِكَاحًا فَلَيْسَ عَلَيْهِنَّ جُنَاحٌ أَن يَضَعْنَ ثِيَابَهُنَّ غَيْرَ مُتَبَرِّجَـٰتٍۭ بِزِينَةٍ ۖ وَأَن يَسْتَعْفِفْنَ خَيْرٌ لَّهُنَّ ۗ وَٱللَّهُ سَمِيعٌ عَلِيمٌ

Now Allah ﷻ addresses elderly women. وَٱلْقَوَٰعِدُ مِنَ ٱلنِّسَآءِ—"The sitting women"—refers to elderly women, those who have passed the age of marriage, who are no longer in the phase of life where marriage is expected or desired. According to most scholars, this refers to women who are quite elderly—grandmothers, great-grandmothers.

ٱلَّـٰتِى لَا يَرْجُونَ نِكَاحًا—"Those who no longer hope for marriage"—they're past that stage of life.

فَلَيْسَ عَلَيْهِنَّ جُنَاحٌ أَن يَضَعْنَ ثِيَابَهُنَّ—"There is no harm on them that they put aside their outer garments." In other words, they don't have to observe the same level of hijab that younger women do. If an elderly woman with gray or white hair, perhaps walking with a hunchback, doesn't cover her hair, that's permissible for her.

But then comes an important caveat: غَيْرَ مُتَبَرِّجَـٰتٍۭ بِزِينَةٍ—"Without displaying their adornment in a showy way." The word تبرج comes from برج , meaning tower—something that's prominent and attention-grabbing. So even these elderly women who have the option to dress more casually shouldn't dress provocatively or in a way that draws excessive attention. They shouldn't wear outrageous colors, excessive jewelry, or anything designed to make them the center of attention.

وَأَن يَسْتَعْفِفْنَ خَيْرٌ لَّهُنَّ—"And if they still observe modesty, it's better for them." Even though Allah ﷻ has given them this concession, if an elderly woman chooses to maintain full hijab, to continue covering as she did in her youth, that's actually more dignified for her. She has the option, but maintaining modesty is encouraged.

وَٱللَّهُ سَمِيعٌ عَلِيمٌ—"And Allah is All-Hearing, All-Knowing."

translation: There is not upon the blind [any] constraint nor upon the lame constraint nor upon the ill constraint nor upon yourselves when you eat from your [own] houses or the houses of your fathers or the houses of your mothers or the houses of your brothers or the houses of your sisters or the houses of your father's brothers or the houses of your father's sisters or the houses of your mother's brothers or the houses of your mother's sisters or [from houses] whose keys you possess or [from the house] of your friend. There is no blame upon you whether you eat together or separately. But when you enter houses, give greetings of peace upon each other - a greeting from Allah, blessed and good. Thus does Allah make clear to you the verses [of ordinance] that you may understand
commentary:
لَّيْسَ عَلَى ٱلْأَعْمَىٰ حَرَجٌ وَلَا عَلَى ٱلْأَعْرَجِ حَرَجٌ وَلَا عَلَى ٱلْمَرِيضِ حَرَجٌ وَلَا عَلَىٰٓ أَنفُسِكُمْ أَن تَأْكُلُوا۟ مِنۢ بُيُوتِكُمْ أَوْ بُيُوتِ ءَابَآئِكُمْ أَوْ بُيُوتِ أُمَّهَـٰتِكُمْ أَوْ بُيُوتِ إِخْوَٰنِكُمْ أَوْ بُيُوتِ أَخَوَٰتِكُمْ أَوْ بُيُوتِ أَعْمَـٰمِكُمْ أَوْ بُيُوتِ عَمَّـٰتِكُمْ أَوْ بُيُوتِ أَخْوَٰلِكُمْ أَوْ بُيُوتِ خَـٰلَـٰتِكُمْ أَوْ مَا مَلَكْتُم مَّفَاتِحَهُۥٓ أَوْ صَدِيقِكُمْ ۚ لَيْسَ عَلَيْكُمْ جُنَاحٌ أَن تَأْكُلُوا۟ جَمِيعًا أَوْ أَشْتَاتًا ۚ فَإِذَا دَخَلْتُم بُيُوتًا فَسَلِّمُوا۟ عَلَىٰٓ أَنفُسِكُمْ تَحِيَّةً مِّنْ عِندِ ٱللَّهِ مُبَـٰرَكَةً طَيِّبَةً ۚ كَذَٰلِكَ يُبَيِّنُ ٱللَّهُ لَكُمُ ٱلْـَٔايَـٰتِ لَعَلَّكُمْ تَعْقِلُونَ

This comprehensive ayah addresses social life, family relationships, and basic etiquette. It begins by removing barriers: لَّيْسَ عَلَى ٱلْأَعْمَىٰ حَرَجٌ—"There is no restriction upon the blind person." Women don't have to wear hijab around a blind man—this is obvious. وَلَا عَلَى ٱلْأَعْرَجِ حَرَجٌ—"Nor upon the disabled person." If you're caring for a disabled family member, certain restrictions are eased. وَلَا عَلَى ٱلْمَرِيضِ حَرَجٌ—"Nor upon the chronically ill." This isn't referring to someone with a cold, but someone who is bedridden, perpetually sick, unable to care for themselves.

Then the ayah addresses where you can eat: وَلَا عَلَىٰٓ أَنفُسِكُمْ أَن تَأْكُلُوا۟ مِنۢ بُيُوتِكُمْ—"And there's no harm upon you to eat in your own homes." When all these restrictions about covering and modesty were revealed, some people worried: "Does this mean we can only eat in our own homes? We can't socialize anywhere else?"

Allah ﷻ clarifies: you can eat in your own homes, or in the homes of your fathers, mothers, brothers, sisters. Then He specifies: أَوْ بُيُوتِ أَعْمَـٰمِكُمْ—your paternal uncles' homes, أَوْ بُيُوتِ عَمَّـٰتِكُمْ—your paternal aunts' homes, أَوْ بُيُوتِ أَخْوَٰلِكُمْ—your maternal uncles' homes, أَوْ بُيُوتِ خَـٰلَـٰتِكُمْ—your maternal aunts' homes.

The Arabic here is beautifully precise. Your paternal uncle is عم , your paternal aunt is عمة , your maternal uncle is خال, and your maternal aunt is خالة . Most English speakers who know these terms only got one right: "khalah" for maternal aunt. The rest we usually pronounce incorrectly.

أَوْ مَا مَلَكْتُم مَّفَاتِحَهُۥٓ—"Or whatever places you own the keys to." If you have multiple properties, second homes, vacation homes, you can go there and be comfortable without observing different rules just because you're not in your "primary" residence.

أَوْ صَدِيقِكُمْ—"Or your friend's house." Sisters can go to a friend's house, relax, take off their hijab if there are no non-mahram men present. There's no harm in this. You're among friends, among family.

لَيْسَ عَلَيْكُمْ جُنَاحٌ أَن تَأْكُلُوا۟ جَمِيعًا أَوْ أَشْتَاتًا—"There's no harm on you whether you eat together or separately." This phrase has sparked classical debate and modern discussion. Some interpret this as permission for mixed-gender gatherings for meals; others understand it differently. The details of this interpretation require careful scholarly discussion, which is better left to specialists in Islamic law.

But then comes a beautiful command that everyone can implement: فَإِذَا دَخَلْتُم بُيُوتًا فَسَلِّمُوا۟ عَلَىٰٓ أَنفُسِكُمْ—"When you enter homes, then greet yourselves." This means two things. First, when you enter someone's home, these are your people—your Muslim brothers and sisters—so they're an extension of yourself. Say salaam to them. Second, even when you enter your own home when nobody's there, you should still say "Assalamu alaikum" to yourself.

That's why people say salaam when they walk into an empty house. Non-Muslims might find it odd—"What are you doing? Nobody's here"—but actually, you're following the Quran. Greet yourselves.

تَحِيَّةً مِّنْ عِندِ ٱللَّهِ مُبَـٰرَكَةً طَيِّبَةً—"A greeting from Allah Himself, blessed and good." This simple act of saying "Assalamu alaikum" when you walk into your house—even if nobody's home—injects blessings into that space. It brings barakah, increases goodness, purifies the environment. The Prophet ﷺ taught us that saying salaam when entering brings angels into the home and drives out shaytan.

Just saying "Assalamu alaikum" when you walk through the door becomes an act of worship. You get reward. Your house becomes filled with barakah. Then, after you've said salaam, you can be yourself. You can act weird, goofy, relaxed—as they say, a man in his own home becomes like a child. But not until you say salaam first.

كَذَٰلِكَ يُبَيِّنُ ٱللَّهُ لَكُمُ ٱلْـَٔايَـٰتِ لَعَلَّكُمْ تَعْقِلُونَ—"That is how Allah clarifies His miraculous signs for you, so that you may understand."

And with this, we complete our study of Surah An-Nur.
